Deep cervical plexus block — greater analgesic depth but higher risk (phrenic nerve, vertebral artery) Surgeon-delivered wound infiltration

Transfemoral amputation for peripheral vascular disease, diabetic complications, trauma, or malignancy. High postoperative pain burden — stump pain, phantom limb pain, and phantom sensation are common. Perioperative regional anaesthesia may reduce the incidence and severity of chronic phantom pain.

Sciatic catheter Targets: Sciatic nerve — tibial and common peroneal components (major innervation of the leg below the knee and posterior thigh) Approach: Ultrasound-guided, popliteal (if feasible given amputation level) or subgluteal/parasacral approach Loading dose: 20 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Infusion: Ropivacaine 0.2% at 5–8 mL/hr with 5 mL patient bolus, 30 min lockout Duration: 48–72 hours minimum — consider longer for phantom pain prophylaxis Femoral nerve / fascia iliaca block Covers the anterior thigh and medial stump. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Single shot usually sufficient; catheter if anterior pain is a significant issue.
documents.s: Recommended: Sciatic nerve catheter (popliteal or subgluteal) + single-shot femoral or fascia iliaca block

documents.t: Lumbar plexus block — covers femoral and obturator nerve territories more completely; higher risk profile Spinal / epidural anaesthesia — useful as primary anaesthetic in high-risk vascular patients; epidural catheter can provide ongoing postoperative analgesia Perineural catheter placed by surgeon — the surgeon can place a catheter directly on the transected sciatic nerve under direct vision

Sciatic nerve: L4–S3, exits pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen, descends between greater trochanter and ischial tuberosity For AKA, the nerve is transected at the level of amputation — the proximal stump is the source of stump and phantom pain Place the catheter as close to the transection level as feasible, or more proximally for broader coverage

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Good pain control including reduced rate of phantom pain; reduced opioid requirement; avoid potential morbidity/mortality of GA in severely co-morbid patients Catheter dislodgement or fallout — may need tunnelling Pneumothorax

Detail Needle Operator preference (Pajunk) Catheter Straight; consider tunnelling for longer duration Pump CADD Solis (grey) Infusion Levobupivacaine 0.125% — max 4 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 6 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration Non-tunnelled: up to 72 hours. Tunnelled: up to 2 weeks Stimulating nerve catheters should be tunnelled and stitched with ≥ 3 cm exposed before securing at the lock device hub.

Phantom limb pain affects 50–80% of amputees; perioperative sciatic catheter infusion for ≥ 48 hours may reduce incidence and severity Starting the block before surgery (pre-emptive analgesia) may be more effective than postoperative block alone These patients are often high-risk (vascular disease, diabetes, renal impairment) — regional anaesthesia reduces opioid burden and haemodynamic instability Anticoagulation: many vascular patients are anticoagulated — check safe block intervals Multimodal approach essential: gabapentinoids (pregabalin/gabapentin) started perioperatively for neuropathic pain prophylaxis

NYSORA: Sciatic Nerve Block NYSORA: Popliteal Sciatic Block NYSORA: Fascia Iliaca Block Bosanquet DC et al. Ann Vasc Surg. 2015 PROSPECT — no specific guideline for amputation; expert consensus applies

Open reduction and internal fixation of ankle fractures — lateral malleolus (fibula), medial malleolus (tibia), posterior malleolus, or combination (bimalleolar/trimalleolar). Tourniquet typically applied to the thigh or calf.

Popliteal sciatic block Targets: Tibial and common peroneal nerves proximal to their bifurcation in the popliteal fossa Approach: Ultrasound-guided, lateral or posterior approach, at or above the sciatic nerve bifurcation Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Onset: 15–30 minutes (sciatic nerve is large — allow adequate onset time) Saphenous nerve block (adductor canal) Targets: Saphenous nerve — sensory supply to the medial malleolus and medial ankle Approach: Ultrasound-guided, mid-thigh at the adductor canal Local anaesthetic: 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375%
documents.s: Popliteal sciatic nerve block + saphenous nerve block

documents.t: Ankle block — ring block of the 5 terminal nerves at the ankle (tibial, deep peroneal, superficial peroneal, sural, saphenous). Suitable for foot surgery; less reliable for ankle ORIF with proximal incisions. Spinal anaesthesia — appropriate as primary anaesthetic, particularly for emergency cases

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Good pain control; improved day case rate; with/without general anaesthesia Transient period of non-weight bearing (may need physiotherapy review ± crutches prior to discharge) —

Sciatic nerve bifurcates into tibial and common peroneal nerves in the popliteal fossa, typically 5–8 cm above the popliteal crease Block proximal to the bifurcation for reliable coverage of both branches Saphenous nerve is a pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve — supplies the medial malleolus via the infrapatellar branch

Sciatic nerve blocks have a slower onset than brachial plexus blocks — allow 30 minutes before assessing Popliteal block provides excellent postoperative analgesia (12–24 hours) Warn patients about foot drop during block duration — provide walking aids and advise fall prevention Thigh tourniquet tolerance requires sedation or GA; calf tourniquet is usually well tolerated with block alone Catheter insertion at the popliteal fossa is feasible for prolonged analgesia
NYSORA: Popliteal Sciatic Block NYSORA: Saphenous (Adductor Canal) Block Provenzano DA et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2005

Elective and emergency foot surgery — hallux valgus correction (bunion surgery), metatarsal osteotomy, midfoot fusion, toe amputation, debridement, tendon repair. Often performed as day case under regional anaesthesia with or without general anaesthesia.

Targets: Five terminal nerves at the ankle — tibial, deep peroneal, superficial peroneal, sural, and saphenous nerves Approach: Ultrasound-guided or landmark-based, circumferential block at the level of the malleoli Local anaesthetic: 3–5 mL levobupivacaine 0.5% per nerve (total 15–25 mL) Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 8–16 hours

Simple, safe, and well-tolerated technique Can be performed with or without ultrasound No motor block above the ankle — patients mobilise more easily Excellent for day case foot surgery Low complication rate
documents.s: Advantages of ankle block for foot surgery

documents.t: Popliteal sciatic nerve block ± saphenous nerve block — provides more reliable and complete anaesthesia of the foot. Better for procedures extending proximal to the ankle or requiring a calf tourniquet. See Ankle ORIF. Local infiltration — surgeon-delivered, suitable for minor procedures (e.g. toenail surgery, small skin lesions) Digital nerve blocks — for single toe procedures

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Good pain control; improved day case rate; with/without general anaesthesia Transient period of non-weight bearing (may need physiotherapy review ± crutches prior to discharge) —

Tibial nerve — posterior to the medial malleolus, deep to flexor retinaculum, alongside posterior tibial artery. Supplies the sole of the foot. Deep peroneal nerve — anterior to the ankle, between extensor hallucis longus and extensor digitorum longus tendons, lateral to the dorsalis pedis artery. Supplies the first web space. Superficial peroneal nerve — subcutaneous on the anterolateral ankle. Supplies the dorsum of the foot. Sural nerve — between the lateral malleolus and Achilles tendon. Supplies the lateral foot. Saphenous nerve — subcutaneous, anterior to the medial malleolus. Supplies the medial ankle.

Ankle block is classified as a superficial block — can generally be performed without cessation of anticoagulants (see Anticoagulation & Blocks) For more proximal procedures (ankle ORIF, Achilles tendon repair), a popliteal sciatic block provides better coverage Warn patients about numbness and difficulty weight-bearing — provide walking aids and fall prevention advice Patients can often be discharged with a functioning block — provide written information on limb protection and analgesia for the wear-off period
NYSORA: Ankle Block Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)
Total hip replacement (THR) or hemiarthroplasty for osteoarthritis, fractured neck of femur, or avascular necrosis. Posterior, lateral, or anterior surgical approaches.

Targets: Femoral nerve, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, ± obturator nerve Approach: Ultrasound-guided, suprainguinal approach — needle inserted lateral to the femoral artery, directed to lift the fascia iliaca off iliacus Local anaesthetic: 30–40 mL ropivacaine 0.375% Onset: 20–30 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

PENG block (pericapsular nerve group) — targets articular branches of the femoral, obturator, and accessory obturator nerves. Ultrasound-guided injection between psoas tendon and superior pubic ramus. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Motor-sparing — may preserve quadriceps strength better than fascia iliaca block. Lumbar plexus block (psoas compartment block) — most complete coverage of hip innervation. Higher risk: epidural spread, retroperitoneal haematoma. Typically reserved for specialist centres. Spinal anaesthesia — commonly used as primary anaesthetic for hip arthroplasty. Intrathecal opioid (e.g. diamorphine 0.1–0.3 mg) provides excellent early postoperative analgesia.

Hip joint innervation: femoral nerve (anterior capsule), obturator nerve (inferomedial capsule), superior gluteal nerve, nerve to quadratus femoris (posterior capsule) Fascia iliaca compartment: between fascia iliaca and iliacus muscle Suprainguinal approach achieves more reliable lateral femoral cutaneous nerve block than infrainguinal

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Reduced opioid dosing and avoidance of opioid side effects; may be topped up for theatre (NOF fixation) if catheter sited Will not be total pain relief; catheter dislodgement or fallout (± re-site) Apnoea

Detail Needle Tuohy needle (Pajunk) Catheter Straight Pump CADD Solis (grey) Infusion Levobupivacaine 0.125% — max 6 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration 24 hours post-fixation (RVI standard) Levobupivacaine 0.125% may not provide adequate analgesia for severe fracture pain — consider 0.25% levobupivacaine or ropivacaine.

Fascia iliaca block is the most common perioperative nerve block for hip fractures (NICE guidelines) PENG block is motor-sparing but may not cover the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (lateral thigh incision pain) Posterior surgical approach: consider adding a lateral femoral cutaneous nerve component For neck of femur fractures, early fascia iliaca block reduces opioid requirements and delirium risk Anticoagulation must be considered — fascia iliaca is relatively compressible; lumbar plexus block is not

NYSORA: Fascia Iliaca Block Steenberg J, Møller AM. Acta Anaesthesiol Scand. 2018 Girón-Arango L et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2018 (original PENG description) NICE guideline CG124: Hip fracture management

Lower limb vascular surgery — femoral-popliteal bypass, femoral-distal bypass, femoral embolectomy, angioplasty with surgical access, wound debridement in PVD. These patients are often high-risk with significant comorbidities (cardiovascular disease, diabetes, renal impairment, respiratory disease). Regional anaesthesia can avoid the risks of general anaesthesia in this population.

Sciatic nerve block Targets: Sciatic nerve — tibial and common peroneal components Approach: Ultrasound-guided, subgluteal or popliteal approach depending on surgical site Local anaesthetic: 20 mL levobupivacaine 0.5% Onset: 15–30 minutes Femoral nerve block Targets: Femoral nerve — anterior thigh and groin incision Approach: Ultrasound-guided, inguinal crease Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL levobupivacaine 0.25–0.5%
documents.s: Recommended: Sciatic nerve block + femoral nerve block

documents.t: Avoids potential morbidity/mortality associated with general anaesthesia in severely co-morbid patients Good pain control including reduced rate of phantom pain (for amputations) Reduced opioid requirement Sympathetic blockade may improve peripheral blood flow May involve catheter insertion for ongoing analgesia

Neuraxial techniques — spinal or epidural anaesthesia. Effective but carries higher haemodynamic impact. Epidural catheter provides excellent ongoing analgesia. Fascia iliaca block — alternative to femoral nerve block with potentially broader coverage; suprainguinal approach preferred. Combined sciatic + fascia iliaca — provides near-complete lower limb coverage. For above knee amputation specifically, see the dedicated Above Knee Amputation page with catheter setup details.

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Good pain control including reduced rate of phantom pain; reduced opioid requirement; avoid potential morbidity/mortality of GA in severely co-morbid patients Horner's syndrome / phrenic nerve block (upper limb only); catheter dislodgement or fallout — need for tunnelling Pneumothorax

Detail Needle Operator preference (Pajunk) Catheter Straight; tunnelling recommended for longer duration Pump CADD Solis (grey) Infusion Levobupivacaine 0.125% — max 4 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 6 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration Non-tunnelled: up to 72 hours. Tunnelled: up to 2 weeks

Sciatic nerve: L4–S3, exits pelvis through greater sciatic foramen; bifurcates into tibial and common peroneal in the popliteal fossa Femoral nerve: L2–L4, lateral to the femoral artery below the inguinal ligament For femoral-popliteal bypass: groin incision requires femoral nerve coverage; below-knee incision requires sciatic nerve coverage

Many vascular patients are anticoagulated — check timing with Anticoagulation & Blocks. Femoral and popliteal sciatic blocks are classified as superficial and can generally be performed without cessation of anticoagulants. Pre-existing peripheral neuropathy (diabetic, vascular) may make nerves more susceptible to injury — document pre-existing deficits before the block Consider pre-emptive analgesia (block before surgery) for amputation cases to reduce phantom pain Gabapentinoids (pregabalin/gabapentin) should be considered perioperatively for neuropathic pain prophylaxis in amputation cases Regional anaesthesia may be particularly valuable in patients with severe cardiorespiratory disease who are high-risk for GA
NYSORA: Sciatic Nerve Block NYSORA: Fascia Iliaca Block Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)
Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) for osteoarthritis, inflammatory arthritis, or post-traumatic arthritis. High postoperative pain scores — multimodal analgesia is essential.

Adductor canal block Targets: Saphenous nerve, nerve to vastus medialis, medial retinacular nerve Approach: Ultrasound-guided, mid-thigh, deep to sartorius Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.2–0.375% Advantage: Motor-sparing — preserves quadriceps strength for early mobilisation iPACK (Interspace between Popliteal Artery and posterior Capsule of the Knee) Targets: Posterior genicular nerves Approach: Ultrasound-guided, needle placed between popliteal artery and posterior femoral condyle Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.2%

Femoral nerve block — excellent analgesia but causes quadriceps weakness (fall risk) Continuous adductor canal catheter — for extended analgesia over 24–48 hours Periarticular local infiltration (LIA) — surgeon-delivered, complements regional techniques

Adductor canal: deep to sartorius, bounded by vastus medialis and adductor longus/magnus Saphenous nerve travels with femoral artery within the canal iPACK target zone: tissue plane posterior to distal femur, between popliteal artery and bone

Adductor canal block preferred over femoral nerve block — equivalent analgesia with preserved motor function iPACK addresses posterior knee pain not covered by adductor canal block alone Combine with paracetamol, NSAIDs, and gabapentinoids as part of enhanced recovery
NYSORA: Saphenous (Adductor Canal) Block Terkawi AS et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2017 Sankineani SR et al. J Arthroplasty. 2018
Lower segment caesarean section (LSCS) via a Pfannenstiel incision. May be elective or emergency (categories 1–4). Regional anaesthesia is the preferred technique for both mother and baby.

Level: L3/4 or L4/5 interspace Local anaesthetic: Hyperbaric bupivacaine 0.5% 2.2–2.6 mL (11–13 mg) Opioid adjuvant: Diamorphine 0.3–0.4 mg or preservative-free morphine 0.1–0.15 mg intrathecally Target block height: T4 to cold/touch Onset: 5–10 minutes
Existing epidural: Rapid top-up with 15–20 mL lidocaine 2% with adrenaline 1:200,000 ± sodium bicarbonate No epidural in situ: Spinal anaesthesia if time permits; general anaesthesia if not
documents.s: For emergency category 1 (immediate threat to life)

documents.t: TAP block — useful when intrathecal opioid is omitted or inadequate. Bilateral subcostal or lateral approach. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side. Provides somatic wound analgesia for 12–18 hours. Quadratus lumborum (QL) block — posterior or lateral approach. May provide more extensive and longer-lasting analgesia than TAP. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375% per side. Wound infiltration / catheter — surgeon-placed subfascial catheter with local anaesthetic infusion

Spinal anaesthesia at L3/4 is below the conus medullaris (ends at L1/2) Pfannenstiel incision: innervated by T11–L1 intercostal and iliohypogastric/ilioinguinal nerves TAP block target: between internal oblique and transversus abdominis

Intrathecal diamorphine/morphine provides excellent analgesia for 12–24 hours post-LSCS — this is the single most effective analgesic intervention Monitor for delayed respiratory depression with intrathecal opioids (especially morphine) Aortocaval compression: left lateral tilt must be maintained until delivery Hypotension is common after spinal — phenylephrine infusion is standard prophylaxis NICE and OAA/AAGBI guidelines recommend regional over general anaesthesia wherever possible
NYSORA: Epidural Anaesthesia and Analgesia NYSORA: TAP Block Kinsella SM et al. Anaesthesia. 2018 (OAA/AAGBI guidelines) Sultan P et al. Br J Anaesth. 2020 NICE guideline CG132: Caesarean birth
Intra-operative re-dosing of antibiotic prophylaxis for adult patients with normal renal function (creatinine in normal range). Based on local guideline (v2, May 2017, re-ratified June 2025).

Re-dose at the standard prophylactic dose (same dose as at induction) if either criterion is met: Operation duration ≥ 4 hours (from knife-to-skin to skin closure), OR Blood loss ≥ 1500 mL (assuming weight 50–100 kg) warning Always re-dose with the same dose you gave at the beginning of the procedure. See the Trust Guide to Antibiotic Prophylaxis for procedure-specific agents and doses.

These antibiotics should be re-dosed at the interval below, or after ≥ 1500 mL blood loss — whichever comes first. Interval Antibiotics 4 hourly Amoxicillin, Aztreonam, Cefuroxime, Co-amoxiclav, Flucloxacillin, Meropenem, Piperacillin/tazobactam, Temocillin 6 hourly Clindamycin 8 hourly Metronidazole
documents.s: Re-dose by time OR blood loss

documents.t: These antibiotics should only be re-dosed for blood loss ≥ 1500 mL — not by time. Interval Antibiotics Blood loss ≥ 1500 mL only Ciprofloxacin, Co-trimoxazole, Ertapenem, Gentamicin (max. once per procedure), Linezolid, Teicoplanin, Tigecycline, Vancomycin
Antifungals do not need to be re-dosed intra-operatively Gentamicin should only be re-dosed a maximum of once per surgical procedure

≥ 1500 mL (weight 50–100 kg): re-dose at the standard prophylactic dose ≥ 3000 mL: re-dose for every 1500 mL blood lost (i.e. re-dose again after each further 1500 mL) Extremes of weight (< 50 kg or > 100 kg): consider re-dosing if blood loss exceeds 20 mL/kg

If using cell saver technology and blood loss ≥ 1500 mL: If cells are washed and spun as part of the process → re-dose the antibiotic The washing process removes the antibiotic from the salvaged blood

Time the re-dose interval from the first dose (at induction), not from the start of surgery Set a timer or write the re-dose time on the whiteboard If antibiotic therapy is to be continued post-operatively, revert to the usual dosing interval (see BNF or contact microbiology) Document re-dosing on the anaesthetic chart

Local Guideline on Intra-Operative Re-Dosing of Antibiotic Prophylaxis for Adult Patients with Normal Renal Function (v2, May 2017, re-ratified June 2025) Scottish Antimicrobial Prescribing Group: Recommendations for Re-dosing Antibiotics for Surgical Prophylaxis (2016) NICE Clinical Guideline 74: Surgical site infections (2008) Bratzler DW et al. ASHP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Surgery. Am J Health-Syst Pharm. 2013; 70: 195-283

Timing of nerve blocks in anticoagulated patients, based on ESRA 2022 guidelines and local guidance. :::warning Key Principle The classification of a block as deep or superficial determines whether anticoagulation timing rules apply. Superficial blocks may generally be performed without cessation of anticoagulants. Deep blocks require consideration of timing from last dose. :::

Based on ESRA 2022 guidelines, reformatted from local guideline Appendix B. Deep nerve blocks Superficial nerve blocks Stellate ganglion, deep cervical plexus, cervical paravertebral Occipital, peribulbar, sub-Tenon's, superficial cervical plexus Infraclavicular Interscalene, supraclavicular, axillary, suprascapular, forearm and wrist blocks Epidural, thoracic paravertebral ESP, serratus plane, parasternal intercostal, interpectoral and pecto-serratus (PECS) Ilioinguinal, TAP, rectus sheath, pudendal Quadratus lumborum, lumbar plexus, psoas compartment, lumbar paravertebral, sacral plexus, proximal sciatic, spinal, epidural Femoral, adductor canal, sciatic (subgluteal or popliteal level only), fascia iliaca, LCNT, ankle blocks

Reproduced and rephrased from ESRA 2022 guidelines (local guideline Appendix C). Drug / dose Time from last dose to block Target lab value Time from block to next dose Warfarin Until INR normal (usually 5 days) Normal range for patient — DOAC (rivaroxaban, edoxaban, apixaban) — CrCl > 30 48h No testing required 24h DOAC — CrCl < 30 72h No testing required 24h LMWH prophylactic (e.g. tinzaparin ≤ 50 units/kg/day) 12h (24h if CrCl < 30) No testing required — LMWH therapeutic 24h (48h if CrCl < 30) Anti-Xa ≤ 0.1 IU/mL — UFH — lower dose (≤ 100 IU/kg/day IV) 4h No testing required — UFH — higher dose Until target lab value (~6h if IV) aPTT or anti-Xa in normal range — Fondaparinux ≤ 2.5 mg/day 36h (72h if CrCl < 50) No testing required — Fondaparinux higher dose Until target lab value (~4 days) Calibrated anti-Xa ≤ 0.1 IU/mL — Aspirin None — can be performed immediately No testing Aspirin can resume at any point P2Y12 inhibitor Ticagrelor: 5 days; Clopidogrel: 5–7 days; Prasugrel: 2 days (or until lab value) None routinely, consider platelet function testing Clopidogrel 75 mg: 0h; Clopidogrel 300 mg: 2 days; Prasugrel/ticagrelor: 1 day Aspirin + antiplatelet/anticoagulant As per the antiplatelet/anticoagulant As per antiplatelet/anticoagulant Aspirin can resume at any point :::info Superficial blocks Superficial nerve blocks may all be performed without cessation of anticoagulant or antithrombotic drugs, provided any relevant level (INR, anti-Xa) is within or below the therapeutic range, and there is no clinical suspicion of overdose or drug accumulation. :::
documents.s: Timing of deep nerve blocks with anticoagulants

documents.t: The ESRA guidelines separate blocks into deep and superficial according to risk of provoking non-compressible or catastrophic bleeding For deep blocks in anticoagulated patients: consider timing carefully, document rationale, and discuss with supervising consultant if performed by a trainee Performing a block that contravenes ESRA timing (e.g. for acute trauma) must be a documented risk-benefit decision in the patient's notes and consent form Therapeutic LMWH should not be given when a nerve or epidural catheter is in situ Trainees performing blocks on anticoagulated patients must have appropriate supervision

ESRA: Regional anaesthesia in patients on antithrombotic drugs. Eur J Anaesthesiol 2022; 39: 100-132 AAGBI: Regional anaesthesia and patients with abnormalities of coagulation. Anaesthesia 2013; 68: 966-72 Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)

For anaesthetic staff managing nerve catheter problems. Based on Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Block Catheters in Adults (v2, February 2024). warning These troubleshooting guides are for anaesthetic staff only — not for non-anaesthetic medical or nursing staff on the wards.

Check the cutaneous block with ethyl chloride spray; assess motor block (or inspiratory spirometry for rib fractures) Ensure no disconnections and the infusion rate is correct and running Is the catheter still in situ? No → Re-site or prescribe alternative analgesia and inform pain team Yes → Assess the block: Is there an adequate block? Inadequate → Bolus 10–20 mL of 0.25% levobupivacaine. Re-site if ineffective. Dense block but inadequate cover → Consider bolus. Consider adding alternate analgesia. For rib fractures: consider siting another PVB at a different level, or switching to an epidural if further coverage needed.

This is common — insertion needles are wider than the catheter. Is the catheter at the same depth as insertion? No → Re-site or prescribe alternative analgesia and inform pain team Yes → Check for subcutaneous collection: Subcutaneous collection present? Yes → Re-site or prescribe alternative analgesia and inform pain team No → Apply Dermabond to the insertion site and a new dressing

Check the giving set — is it clamped or incorrectly connected? Yes → Unclamp, check NRFit connections are correct, continue infusion Check the catheter — is it kinked or stretched? Kinked → Check aseptically under the dressing, unkink. Consider sterile Steri-Strip to avoid further kinks. Stretched → If there is length, cut the stretched segment with sterile scissors and reconnect. Blocked catheter — attempt to inject 0.9% sodium chloride (aseptic technique): Able to flush → Check infusion is running. If yes, resolved. Unable to flush → Plan to re-site or prescribe alternative analgesia

danger If signs of LA toxicity (metallic taste, confusion, dizziness, tinnitus): Stop the infusion immediately ABC assessment. Consider moving patient to a monitored area (e.g. Recovery). Inform pain team. Any CVS/neurological impairment? Yes → Follow the AAGBI Management of Severe LA Toxicity guideline No → Check for blood on aspiration: Blood on aspiration? Yes → Withdraw catheter by 1–2 cm and aspirate again. If no blood, restart infusion in a monitored area. If still blood, re-site or prescribe alternative. Inform pain team. No → Once symptoms resolve, restart infusion at a lower rate with continual monitoring. Give instructions to stop if symptoms recur.

Disconnect proximal to filter (between filter and nerve catheter)? Yes + witnessed/informed immediately → Spray catheter with 0.5% chlorhexidine, cut the tip by 1 cm, restart infusion Yes + not witnessed immediately + visibly soiled → Re-site or prescribe alternative, inform pain team Yes + not witnessed immediately + not visibly soiled → Spray with 0.5% chlorhexidine, cut tip by 1 cm, restart infusion, inform pain team No (distal to filter) → Change the giving set and reconnect to the filter Keep the pump sterile and re-use if the catheter is re-sited.
Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Block Catheters in Adults (v2, February 2024)

Patient refusal Anaphylaxis to the local anaesthetic agent

Anticoagulated patient — see Anticoagulation & Blocks for deep vs superficial classification and ESRA timing Risk of compartment syndrome — block may mask the clinical signs Significant respiratory disease — techniques that risk phrenic nerve palsy (e.g. interscalene, supraclavicular) may cause dangerous respiratory compromise Local infection at the injection site Pre-existing neurological deficit in the distribution of the planned block — document baseline status before proceeding

Discussion of regional anaesthetic techniques should happen as early as possible: Pre-assessment clinic (elective surgery) Plastics trauma clinic / ward 39 pre-assessment Surgical clinics for upper limb, lower limb, breast, and vascular access surgery The technique should be further discussed by the anaesthetist performing the procedure on the day of surgery.

Tailor information to the patient. Cover: Rationale and benefits of the proposed technique What the procedure involves and what to expect Common risks and side effects Serious or life-threatening complications Any significant risk specific to that patient All possible alternatives — the patient must understand these and not feel coerced into regional anaesthesia :::info Appendix A consent tables Each procedure page includes a Consent discussion table summarising benefits, common side effects, and serious complications for that specific block. :::

For day surgery involving regional anaesthesia, specific instructions must include: Practical aspects of the nerve block That sedation may be offered but they will not be under general anaesthetic Analgesia cover for when the block wears off — provide oral analgesia to take home Limb protection — the limb will be numb and weak until normal sensation and motor function return Expected non-threatening physiological changes (e.g. Horner's syndrome with interscalene block) When to seek help — signs of infection at the injection site, or failure of normal sensation/motor function to return beyond the expected duration of the block
Provision of written information is recommended. The RCoA / RA-UK patient information booklet is available, along with specific advice sheets for day case shoulder surgery.

The consent process should always be documented in the patient's notes The AAGBI advises that signed consent is required when the block is not part of another related process (i.e. when it is the sole anaesthetic technique, or for standalone acute pain interventions such as rib fracture blocks) Acute pain or concurrent opioid use does not necessarily impair mental capacity Capacity should be presumed and assessed as per the Mental Capacity Act 2005 In urgent situations, clinicians must balance ensuring the patient has sufficient opportunity to consider treatment options whilst providing analgesia in a timely manner — document this decision-making clearly

Changing a planned anaesthetic technique immediately prior to induction or during a procedure is not best practice. This should only occur in exceptional circumstances (e.g. patient preference changes, pain develops during surgery, an emergency develops).

Local Anaesthetic Max Dose (mg/kg) Max Dose (mg/kg) with Adrenaline Absolute Max (70 kg) Onset Duration Lidocaine 3 7 200 / 500 mg Fast (5 min) 1–2 hr Bupivacaine 2 2 150 mg Moderate (15 min) 4–8 hr Levobupivacaine 2 2 150 mg Moderate (15 min) 4–8 hr Ropivacaine 3 3 200 mg Moderate (15 min) 6–12 hr Prilocaine 6 8 400 / 600 mg Fast (5 min) 1.5–3 hr warning These are maximum doses — not target doses. Use the minimum effective dose. Doses are additive when mixing agents.

Concentration Volume containing 1 mg/mL 20 mL contains 30 mL contains 0.2% 2 mg/mL 40 mg 60 mg 0.375% 3.75 mg/mL 75 mg 112.5 mg 0.5% 5 mg/mL 100 mg 150 mg 0.75% 7.5 mg/mL 150 mg 225 mg Example: 70 kg patient, max ropivacaine = 210 mg. That's 42 mL of 0.5% or 56 mL of 0.375%.
Concentration mg/mL 20 mL contains 30 mL contains 0.25% 2.5 mg/mL 50 mg 75 mg 0.375% 3.75 mg/mL 75 mg 112.5 mg 0.5% 5 mg/mL 100 mg 150 mg
Concentration mg/mL 10 mL contains 20 mL contains 0.5% 5 mg/mL 50 mg 100 mg 1% 10 mg/mL 100 mg 200 mg 2% 20 mg/mL 200 mg 400 mg

Calculate the dose in mg, not volume — a "safe" volume of a high concentration can still be toxic Doses are cumulative — if using multiple blocks or supplementing with local infiltration, add all LA together Patient factors that reduce safe dose: extremes of age, hepatic impairment, cardiac disease, low body weight, pregnancy (more sensitive to cardiotoxicity), hypoalbuminaemia Ropivacaine has a better safety profile than bupivacaine (less cardiotoxic, lower CNS toxicity threshold) Intravascular injection causes toxicity at much lower doses than subcutaneous — always aspirate and use incremental injection Ultrasound guidance reduces the risk of intravascular injection

Adjuvant Dose Effect Notes Adrenaline 1:200,000 (5 mcg/mL) Prolongs block, reduces absorption, marker for intravascular injection Avoid in digital blocks (debated), end-artery territories Dexamethasone (perineural) 4 mg Prolongs block by 6–8 hours Off-label; equivalent evidence for IV route Dexamethasone (IV) 4–8 mg Prolongs block by 4–6 hours Give at time of block Clonidine 1 mcg/kg (max 150 mcg) Modest prolongation Can cause bradycardia, sedation, hypotension
Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) can present within minutes of injection or up to 60 minutes later.
Perioral tingling / numbness Metallic taste Tinnitus / auditory disturbance Dizziness, lightheadedness Agitation, confusion Visual disturbance

Tremor → seizures → loss of consciousness Bradycardia, conduction block Hypotension Ventricular arrhythmias → cardiac arrest :::danger Important Cardiovascular collapse can occur without preceding CNS symptoms — especially with bupivacaine. Bupivacaine cardiotoxicity is particularly resistant to standard resuscitation. :::
Stop LA injection immediately Call for help
Manage airway — 100% oxygen If seizing: midazolam 1–2 mg IV or propofol in small doses Avoid large doses of propofol (cardio-depressant) Intubate if necessary

:::tip Intralipid Protocol (AAGBI guideline) BOLUS: 1.5 mL/kg IV over 1 minute INFUSION: 15 mL/kg/hr IV If cardiovascular instability persists after 5 minutes: Repeat bolus (up to 2 further boluses at 5-minute intervals) Double the infusion rate to 30 mL/kg/hr Maximum cumulative dose: 12 mL/kg For a 70 kg patient: Bolus = 105 mL Infusion = 1050 mL/hr :::

Start CPR — prolonged resuscitation may be required (LA cardiotoxicity can be reversed) Avoid vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and further local anaesthetic (e.g. lidocaine for arrhythmia) Adrenaline: use small doses (< 1 mcg/kg) — large boluses may worsen outcome Amiodarone for ventricular arrhythmias (not lidocaine) Consider cardiopulmonary bypass / ECMO if available and arrest is refractory

Location: Know where Intralipid is stored in your department — it should be immediately accessible in all areas where regional anaesthesia is performed Intralipid comes as 20% solution in 250 mL or 500 mL bags Can be given through a standard IV giving set — does not need a central line Start Intralipid early — do not wait for cardiac arrest Continue Intralipid even if ROSC is achieved — re-arrest is possible

Ultrasound guidance for all peripheral nerve blocks Aspirate before every injection and inject incrementally (3–5 mL aliquots) Use the minimum effective dose of LA Adrenaline as a marker: heart rate increase or T-wave changes suggest intravascular injection Know the maximum dose before drawing up Monitor the patient during and for 30 minutes after injection Have Intralipid immediately available wherever blocks are performed
All cases of LAST should be reported to: Local incident reporting system National reporting: AAGBI LAST registry
AAGBI Safety Guideline: Management of Severe Local Anaesthetic Toxicity (2010, updated 2023) Neal JM et al. ASRA Practice Advisory on LAST. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2020

Pathway for managing unexpected neurological dysfunction after a peripheral nerve block, based on RA-UK/BOA guidelines and local protocol (v3, March 2024). Nerve injury is a recognised but rare complication of regional anaesthesia. Most symptoms resolve in 4–6 weeks in over 95% of patients, and in 99% by one year.

Vascular injury — haematoma at the surgical or block site, disruption of blood supply, DVT. Presents early (minutes to hours). Compression — expanding haematoma, tourniquet/casts, compartment syndrome, improper positioning, increased endoneural fluid pressure after block, trauma/surgery. Direct needle trauma — frequency is greater with long-bevelled needles; duration and severity are greater with short-bevelled needles. Intraneural injection — increases pressure inside the nerve, causing damage. LA neurotoxicity — all local anaesthetics are potentially neurotoxic. Vasoconstrictive additives may affect neural blood supply and potentiate injury. :::info Confounding factors Pre-existing neuropathies (e.g. diabetes), demyelinating diseases (e.g. MS), and peripheral vascular disease may make nerves more susceptible to injury. Document any pre-existing neurological deficits before performing a block. :::

Type Description Recovery Neuropraxia Axons and supporting connective tissue intact Complete recovery expected within weeks to months Axonotmesis Axonal interruption with conservation of connective tissue Generally favourable, although not always complete Neurotmesis Complete fascicular disruption Often poor, even with prompt surgical intervention
All patients receiving a nerve block should be followed up within 48 hours (in person or electronically) to check for neurological dysfunction.

At the 48-hour review, compare post-operative findings with any pre-existing neurological symptoms documented before the block. If pre-operative and post-operative deficits are consistent, document that the deficit was pre-existing.
Signs lasting more than 48 hours after a single-shot block, or any new-onset: Paraesthesia (numbness/tingling) Weakness Unexplained excessive pain
documents.s: If new neurological deficits are detected

documents.t: Inform the responsible surgical and anaesthesia team Review medical, anaesthetic, and surgical records Assess all functions of the nerve — sensory and motor Observe skin for colour changes, swelling; check pulses Consider loosening bandages, splitting plaster of Paris, repositioning the limb

YES — URGENT LESION Is the distribution consistent with the block performed? Yes (likely block-related): Consider compression of nerve (e.g. haematoma). Seek urgent senior surgical and/or neurology consultation. Immediate/urgent surgical decompression may be required. Consider urgent ultrasound/MRI. No (likely surgery-related): Consider compartment syndrome, nerve trauma or compression from suture/bone fragments/haematoma. Seek urgent surgical review. NO — NON-URGENT LESION Is the distribution consistent with the block performed? Yes (likely block-related): Reassure patient. Protect the limb (sling or splint, advise care for numb areas). Consider early involvement of Chronic Pain Team if allodynia or hyperalgesia present (to exclude/treat CRPS). Review in 2–4 weeks — if deficits persist, consider nerve conduction studies, EMG, MRI and refer for local neurology or nerve injury specialist opinion. No (likely surgery-related): Consider positional pressure-related nerve injury, damage at the surgical site, or prolonged/excessive tourniquet. Refer to surgical team. :::tip Neuropathic pain If neuropathic pain is present or pain is limited to the distribution of a particular nerve, refer to the Acute Pain Service and consider starting neuropathic medication (e.g. gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline). ::: :::info Differential diagnosis Post-surgical inflammatory neuropathy should be considered in the differential for both urgent and non-urgent lesions. :::
documents.s: Step 2: Is motor function affected, symptoms progressive, or deficit painful?

documents.t: In hours: APS — nuth.inpatientpainservicervi@nhs.net / DECT 39996 Out of hours: PINC anaesthetist (DECT 29214) Patients will be reviewed by a senior member of the regional anaesthesia team as inpatient or outpatient

RA-UK/BOA: Peripheral nerve block follow up and initial management of postoperative unexpected/persistent neurological dysfunction (2021) Local Guideline: Management of Suspected Nerve Injury after Peripheral Nerve Block (v3, March 2024) Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023) Neal JM et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2015; 40: 401–430 Watson JC, Huntoon MA. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2015; 40(5) Lam KK et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2020; 45: 495–504
Safety checklist and best practice for single-shot peripheral nerve blocks, based on local guidelines.

IV access must be in situ Monitoring: SpO2, non-invasive BP, ECG (AAGBI standard) NR fit equipment is mandatory Resuscitation equipment available — know the location of the nearest Intralipid Consent documented — see Contraindications & Consent for full guidance Anaesthetic assessment completed

Hand washing before commencing Chlorhexidine 0.5% in 70% alcohol — apply via multi-use spray bottle, allow to dry Sterile ultrasound gel for all peripheral techniques Sterile probe cover on transducer ANTT (Aseptic Non-Touch Technique) — key parts: needle tip, catheter, injection port, syringe tips Do not pass the needle tip through the gel Probe decontamination: Tristel Solo wipes after use (avoid Clinell wipes on ultrasound probes unless confirmed compatible) Single-shot blocks do not require the same barrier precautions as neuraxial techniques — minimum is hand washing, skin disinfection, sterile gel, and probe cover
The RCoA/RA-UK/SALG "Stop Before You Block" check is mandatory.
Drugs and equipment ready. Ultrasound scan. Position patient and clean site. Do not open the needle yet.
Just before you block — pause and check: Blocker: "I've completed my prep, let's Stop Before You Block" Assistant: "OK, let's Stop Before You Block" Both: Check mark and consent (check with patient)
Open needle. Immediately perform nerve block. If any delay after the STOP check, restart the process.

If the surgical site is already marked, do not make a separate anaesthetic mark If performing a block before surgery (e.g. fascia iliaca for NOF) and no surgical mark yet: mark the block site with a "B" in a visible location, ideally at the time of consenting For rib fractures: bilateral blocks do not require marking; for unilateral, consider marking the block level If the planned surgical site is unclear, contact the surgical team before proceeding

Inject in maximum 5 mL boluses, aspirating before each Use 10 mL or 20 mL syringes (lower injection pressures) Any event of pain, paraesthesia, blood on aspiration, or difficult injection should prompt needle repositioning and must be documented Communicate with the patient throughout where possible Blocks may be performed awake, under sedation, or under GA — interscalene and supraclavicular are safer performed on awake patients (expert opinion) Trauma patients (e.g. hip or rib fractures) may need supplementary analgesia (fentanyl / alfentanil) to tolerate positioning for catheter insertion If inserting a stimulating nerve catheter under GA, avoid non-depolarising muscle relaxants (or use a short-acting agent) — muscle contraction is needed to guide catheter placement. Consider LMA-to-ETT airway exchange after catheter insertion if relaxation is needed.

Monitor for 30 minutes post-injection (observations recorded) Continue monitoring until the end of any surgical procedure Document in eRecord: Patient's conscious state Asepsis precautions used Ultrasound guidance used Concordance with Stop Before You Block check Needle type and dimensions LA type, volume, and concentration Any complications Post-procedure instructions for the parent team (including intended duration of infusion for catheters) For catheter documentation: Powerchart autotext is available — add a new document in Document View, type @@, and select the appropriate catheter template (e.g. FIC, PVC) An anaesthetist must remain immediately available until the case is complete and patient is transferred

Patients may be anxious about awake surgery or the block itself Consider distraction therapies (ambient music) or sedation VR headsets may be available If sedation is used, it must be administered by a competent practitioner with appropriate monitoring
Report any suspected serious complication to the Regional Anaesthesia Lead Complete a Datix Suspected nerve injury: follow the Nerve Injury Follow-up pathway
Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023) RCoA/RA-UK/SALG: Stop Before You Block NICE IPG285: Ultrasound Guided Regional Nerve Block (2009)
Local loading doses, infusion rates, equipment, and catheter durations. Based on the Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Block Catheters in Adults (v2, February 2024).

Block type 0.25% max volume 0.5% max volume >50 kg / >60 kg / >70 kg >50 kg / >60 kg / >70 kg ESP 30 / 40 / 50 — QL, TAP, Serratus 15/side / 20/side / 25/side — Interscalene 15 / 20 / 25 10 / 15 / 20 Axillary, Supra/Infraclavicular, Sciatic, Femoral 25 / 30 / 40 15 / 20 / 25 Fascia iliaca 30 / 35 / 40 — Paravertebral 30 / 35 / 40 15 / 20 / 25 Rectus sheath 15/side / 20/side / 25/side —

Block type 1% max volume >50 kg / >60 kg / >70 kg ESP 30 / 40 / 50 QL, TAP, Serratus 15/side / 20/side / 25/side Interscalene 15 / 20 / 25 Axillary, Supra/Infraclavicular, Sciatic, Femoral 25 / 30 / 40 Fascia iliaca 25 / 30 / 40 Paravertebral 30 / 35 / 40 Rectus sheath —

Block type Max rate 0.25% (mL/hr) Max rate 0.125% (mL/hr) >50 / >60 / >70 kg >50 / >60 / >70 kg ESP, QL, TAP, Serratus — 6 / 8 / 10 Renal transplant — 5 / 5 / 5 Lower limb (sciatic, femoral) — 4 / 6 / 8 Upper limb (brachial plexus) — 2 / 4 / 4 Fascia iliaca — 6 / 8 / 8 Paravertebral 6 / 8 / 10 — Rectus sheath 4 (2+2) / 4 (2+2) / 4 (2+2) —

Block type Max rate 0.2% (mL/hr) >50 / >60 / >70 kg ESP, QL, TAP, Serratus 10 / 12 / 14 Lower limb (sciatic, femoral) 6 / 8 / 10 Upper limb (brachial plexus) 4 / 6 / 6 Fascia iliaca — Paravertebral 8 / 10 / 12 :::info Notes Ropivacaine 0.2% is commonly used at the RVI, particularly for shoulder surgery. Levobupivacaine 0.125% is available pre-mixed; 0.25% needs to be drawn up from vials (drug error risk). Levobupivacaine 0.125% may not provide adequate analgesia for severe pain (rib fractures, femoral fractures) — consider 0.25% levobupivacaine or ropivacaine. :::

Block Needle Catheter Interscalene Facet needle Straight catheter Paravertebral Tuohy needle Curled catheter Fascia iliaca Tuohy needle Straight catheter Popliteal sciatic / Supraclavicular / Infraclavicular / Superficial cervical plexus Operator preference Straight catheter
Pump Used for CADD Solis (must be grey — yellow pumps are for neuraxial only) Lower limb catheters Elastomeric pump Upper limb catheters, paravertebral catheters

Skin glue at the insertion site to form a barrier and minimise leakage Lockit fixation device to prevent catheter migration Transparent Tegaderm dressing with Hypafix window for inspection Stimulating nerve catheters: tunnel, stitch with ≥ 3 cm exposed before securing at the lock device hub, cover with transparent dressing

Setting Frequency In Recovery Every 15 minutes On ward Hourly for 4 hours, then 4-hourly until discontinued After a bolus dose Every 5 minutes for 30 minutes Observations must include: NEWS 2, pain assessment (at rest and on movement, including ability to deep breathe, cough, mobilise).

LAI exit site: leakage, redness, tenderness, signs of infection Pump and infusion: correct drug prescribed and infusing, pump settings match prescription, CADD Solis connected to mains power, elastomeric pump in dedicated bag IV access must remain patent while any LAI is in situ

Check motor block using the modified Bromage scale at 1 hour after insertion (or in Recovery), 6 hours later, and the following morning. Document on the LAI chart. Grade Description 1 Free movement of leg and foot 2 Just able to flex knee with free movement of foot 3 Unable to flex knee but free movement of foot 4 Unable to move leg or foot danger Bromage score ≥ 2 requires urgent review by IPS / senior anaesthetist. Consider MRI if indicated.

Block Standard duration Notes Fascia iliaca (hip fracture) 24 hours post-fixation Paravertebral (rib fractures) Up to 5 days Interscalene 72 hours Non-tunnelled catheters Up to 72 hours Tunnelled catheters Up to 2 weeks Intended duration should be documented at the time of insertion Infusions must be discontinued within 7 days unless alternative guidance is documented from IPS Infection risk increases with duration — review daily and document rationale for continuation Decisions to continue beyond standard duration should involve experienced decision makers (consultants and pain nurses)
All nerve catheters must be referred to the Acute Pain Team for ongoing management: Enter details on the Pain Database, email nuth.InPatientPainServiceRVI@nhs.net, or call DECT 29912 / 29996

Remove when oral analgesia is established and pain is controlled, or if signs of infection Only remove if the catheter comes out easily Difficulty extracting may indicate tethering or knotting — do not use excessive force Snapped catheter: individual risk/benefit decision (infection risk vs retrieval damage). Inform APS and RA lead. Catheter tip culture: send for C&S only if there are signs of infection at the exit site or the patient is pyrexial — routine culture is not required

Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL Loading dose 5–10 mL bolus, titrated to effect Infusion rate 5–10 mL/hr Patient bolus 3–5 mL, lockout 20–30 min Duration Typically 48–72 hours postoperatively

Assess block height (target: 4 dermatomes above and below incision) Monitor: sedation score, respiratory rate, motor block (Bromage scale), blood pressure If unilateral block: withdraw catheter 1 cm and rebolus If inadequate despite troubleshooting: consider conversion to IV PCA + rescue nerve block

All patients receiving epidural analgesia must have leg strength assessed regularly and recorded on the epidural chart. Profound leg weakness (unable to bend one or both knees, Bromage score 2 or 3) usually indicates the infusion rate is too high — but it may indicate an epidural haematoma. Motor block score 2 or 3? → Stop the epidural infusion and contact the Acute Pain Team Reassess leg strength every 30 minutes Patient can now bend their knees? Yes → Restart epidural infusion. If the patient is uncomfortable, contact APS to reassess analgesia. No, and 4 hours have passed since stopping → Suspect epidural haematoma. Contact APS immediately. danger An epidural haematoma must be evacuated within 8 hours of symptom onset for the best chance of recovery of neurological function. Do not delay escalation.

Parameter Standard Drug Morphine 1 mg/mL Bolus dose 1 mg Lockout 5 minutes Background infusion Not recommended routinely (respiratory depression risk) 4-hour max 20–40 mg (adjust for age, renal function)

Acceptable in some cases (e.g. oesophagectomy, polytrauma) The maximum LA infusion rate is summative — if max calculated rate is 14 mL/h, the sum of both catheter infusion rates must not exceed this Multiple simultaneous nerve catheters may be considered at the RVI

Intravenous lidocaine as a systemic analgesic adjunct for major open or laparoscopic surgery (e.g. bowel resection, nephrectomy, spinal fixation) and rescue analgesia in recovery/critical care. Theatre and critical care areas only — not suitable for ward use. Parameter Standard Loading dose 1.5 mg/kg slow IV push over 10 minutes (max 100 mg) Maintenance 1 mg/kg/hr using undiluted lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL), max 10 mL/hr Duration Max 24 hours unless discussed with APS Weight Use Ideal Body Weight if BMI > 30 (women: height cm − 105; men: height cm − 100) Minimum weight Not for patients < 40 kg Monitoring Continuous ECG mandatory; observations as per LA infusion chart :::danger Critical interaction with regional anaesthesia Patients on IV lidocaine must not receive local anaesthetic by any other route (nerve block, epidural, wound catheter with bolus) 4-hour gap required between IV lidocaine and any nerve or fascial plane block (in either direction) An LA infusion without bolus through a wound or epidural catheter may be started 30 minutes after IV lidocaine is stopped ::: Contraindications: concurrent LA by another route, amide LA hypersensitivity, complete heart block or impaired cardiovascular function, hypovolaemia, weight < 40 kg Cautions: pregnancy, myasthenia gravis, epilepsy, acute porphyria, congestive heart failure, bradycardia, hepatic or renal insufficiency Key interactions: beta-blockers, amiodarone, phenytoin, antiarrhythmic agents, QT-prolonging antipsychotics
Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Block Catheters in Adults (v2, February 2024) Local Guideline for IV Lidocaine for Peri-operative Pain Management in Adults (v5, February 2025)
Open or laparoscopic repair of inguinal hernia. One of the most common general surgical procedures.

TAP block Targets: T10–L1 intercostal nerves within the transversus abdominis plane Approach: Ultrasound-guided, lateral or subcostal approach depending on incision Local anaesthetic: 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side Ilioinguinal / iliohypogastric nerve block Targets: Ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves medial to ASIS Approach: Ultrasound-guided, between internal oblique and transversus abdominis Local anaesthetic: 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375%
documents.s: TAP block (transversus abdominis plane) ± ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric nerve block

documents.t: Spinal anaesthesia — suitable as primary anaesthetic for open repair Surgeon-delivered local infiltration — complements regional block
For open repair, ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric block is often sufficient For laparoscopic repair, TAP block addresses port site and mesh pain Combine with simple oral analgesics for enhanced recovery
NYSORA: TAP Block PROSPECT guidelines for inguinal hernia repair

Midline or transverse laparotomy for bowel resection, emergency laparotomy, hepatobiliary surgery, or gynaecological surgery. One of the most painful surgical procedures — effective regional analgesia is a cornerstone of enhanced recovery.

Level: T8–T10 for mid/lower abdominal surgery; T6–T8 for upper abdominal surgery Catheter: Threaded 3–5 cm into the epidural space Infusion: Ropivacaine 0.2% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL at 5–10 mL/hr Duration: Typically maintained for 48–72 hours postoperatively

Gold standard for open abdominal surgery analgesia Reduces opioid consumption, ileus, and respiratory complications Facilitates early mobilisation and return of gut function Provides bilateral, continuous, titratable analgesia

Rectus sheath block — targets terminal branches of T9–T11 intercostal nerves. 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side, between rectus abdominis and posterior rectus sheath. Useful for midline incisions when epidural is contraindicated. Catheter insertion allows continuous infusion. TAP block (subcostal + lateral) — subcostal TAP for supraumbilical coverage; lateral TAP for infraumbilical. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per injection site. Single-shot duration is limited (~12 hours); catheters improve this. Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — bilateral ESP at T7–T9. Emerging evidence as an epidural alternative. Motor-sparing; potentially less haemodynamic impact. Intrathecal morphine — 0.1–0.3 mg preservative-free morphine added to spinal anaesthetic. Excellent analgesia for 12–24 hours. Appropriate when epidural is not planned (e.g. laparoscopic-assisted cases).

Thoracic epidural space: narrower than lumbar; steep angulation of spinous processes requires a midline approach with significant cephalad needle tilt Rectus sheath: posterior sheath is absent below the arcuate line (below the umbilicus) — relevant for block technique TAP: between internal oblique and transversus abdominis; lateral approach at the mid-axillary line

Epidural contraindicated with coagulopathy, patient refusal, infection at insertion site, or haemodynamic instability Epidural failure rate is 10–30% — have a rescue plan (TAP/rectus sheath block, IV PCA) For emergency laparotomy, time and haemodynamic status may preclude epidural insertion TAP and rectus sheath blocks provide somatic analgesia only (no visceral coverage) — combine with systemic analgesics Enhanced recovery protocols (ERAS) strongly recommend regional anaesthesia for open abdominal surgery
NYSORA: Epidural Anaesthesia and Analgesia NYSORA: TAP Block NYSORA: Erector Spinae Plane Block Pöpping DM et al. Arch Surg. 2008 PROSPECT guidelines for laparotomy ERAS Society guidelines
Simple mastectomy, modified radical mastectomy, wide local excision ± axillary node clearance, breast reconstruction. Significant postoperative pain; chronic pain risk is high (up to 25–50%).

Targets: Ipsilateral thoracic spinal nerves (T2–T6) including dorsal and ventral rami Approach: Ultrasound-guided, parasagittal in-plane at the relevant thoracic levels Local anaesthetic: 4–5 mL ropivacaine 0.5% per level, or 20 mL as a single-injection technique at T3–T4 Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Provides unilateral somatic and sympathetic blockade Equivalent analgesia to thoracic epidural with fewer side effects Evidence for reduction in chronic post-surgical pain Avoids hypotension, urinary retention, and bilateral motor block

Pectoral nerve blocks (PECS I & II) — PECS I: between pectoralis major and minor, targets medial and lateral pectoral nerves. PECS II: between pectoralis minor and serratus anterior at the level of the 4th rib, targets intercostal nerves T2–T6. 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per plane. Simpler and safer than PVB; good for less extensive surgery. Serratus anterior plane (SAP) block — superficial or deep to serratus anterior at the mid-axillary line, 5th rib. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375%. Covers lateral chest wall; useful adjunct for axillary dissection. Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — emerging alternative to PVB with similar dermatomal spread, potentially safer. Evidence base still developing.

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Improved day case rate; reduced opioid dosing and avoidance of opioid side effects; usually performed after induction — Pneumothorax; paravertebral haematoma; epidural spread

Paravertebral space: wedge-shaped, bounded by vertebral body, superior costotransverse ligament, and parietal pleura Breast innervation: anterior and lateral cutaneous branches of T2–T6 intercostal nerves, plus supraclavicular nerves (C3–C4) to upper breast Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves — relevant for axillary dissection

Detail Needle Tuohy needle (Pajunk) Catheter Curled (reduces migration risk) Pump Elastomeric Infusion Levobupivacaine 0.25% — max 6 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 10 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration 48–72 hours

PVB carries a small risk of pneumothorax (< 1% with ultrasound guidance) For bilateral mastectomy, bilateral PVB or thoracic epidural may be considered PECS/SAP blocks are fascial plane blocks — more volume-dependent and may have less consistent medial coverage Catheter-based PVB can be used for ongoing analgesia post-reconstruction Multimodal analgesia essential: paracetamol, NSAIDs, consider dexamethasone and ketamine

NYSORA: Thoracic Paravertebral Block NYSORA: Pectoralis & Serratus Plane Blocks Schnabel A et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2010 Wahba SS, Kamal SM. J Anesth. 2014 PROSPECT guidelines for breast surgery

Not a surgical procedure per se, but rib fractures (traumatic or pathological) cause severe pain that impairs ventilation, coughing, and clearance of secretions. Inadequate analgesia leads to pneumonia, respiratory failure, and prolonged ICU stay. Regional anaesthesia is a key intervention.

Targets: Lateral cutaneous branches of the intercostal nerves (T2–T9) Approach: Ultrasound-guided, superficial to serratus anterior at the mid-axillary line, 4th–5th rib Local anaesthetic: 30 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375% Duration: 8–12 hours (single shot); continuous catheter infusion for ongoing analgesia Advantage: Safe, superficial, easily performed in the emergency department or ICU; low risk of pneumothorax

Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — posterior approach at the level of the affected ribs. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Covers both anterior and posterior rib pain; catheter insertion straightforward. Safe distance from pleura. Paravertebral block (PVB) — more targeted coverage of specific dermatomes. Can be placed as a catheter. Small pneumothorax risk. Thoracic epidural — best evidence for multiple bilateral rib fractures. Requires monitoring and nursing familiarity. Often impractical in polytrauma (coagulopathy, spinal injury). Intercostal nerve blocks — targeted but short-acting unless repeated. Multiple injections needed for multiple fractures. Higher pneumothorax risk per injection.

Serratus anterior overlies the lateral chest wall from ribs 1–9 SAP block targets the fascial plane between serratus anterior and the external intercostal muscles (or superficial to serratus) ESP block: local anaesthetic deposited deep to erector spinae muscle, superficial to transverse processes; spreads to paravertebral space via costotransverse foramina

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Avoidance of pain, respiratory failure, lung infection, and critical care admission Will involve catheter; may require top-ups; regular pain team review; catheter dislodgement or fallout (± re-site) Pneumothorax; paravertebral haematoma; epidural spread

Detail Needle Tuohy needle (Pajunk) Catheter Curled (reduces migration risk) Pump Elastomeric Infusion Levobupivacaine 0.25% — max 6 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 8 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 10 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Cardiothoracics Bupivacaine 0.1% — 8–10 mL/hr (< 70 kg), 12–15 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration Up to 5 days (RVI standard) Levobupivacaine 0.125% may not provide adequate analgesia for rib fracture pain — 0.25% is preferred.

Early aggressive analgesia reduces pneumonia, intubation, and mortality SAP and ESP blocks are safer than epidural and PVB in anticoagulated or coagulopathic trauma patients Catheter-based techniques preferred for ongoing pain management (rib fracture pain peaks at days 2–3) Often combined with systemic multimodal analgesia: paracetamol, NSAIDs, ketamine infusion, lidocaine infusion In elderly patients, rib fractures carry high morbidity — early regional anaesthesia referral is critical

NYSORA: Pectoralis & Serratus Plane Blocks NYSORA: Erector Spinae Plane Block NYSORA: Thoracic Paravertebral Block NICE guideline NG38: Fractures — non-complex May L et al. Anaesthesia. 2016 Thiruvenkatarajan V et al. Anaesth Intensive Care. 2020
Open or endoscopic division of the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) to decompress the median nerve. Usually a short day-case procedure.

Technique: Subcutaneous infiltration of local anaesthetic with adrenaline at the surgical site Local anaesthetic: 10–20 mL lidocaine 1% with 1:100,000 adrenaline Onset: Inject 30 minutes prior to incision (allows adrenaline vasoconstriction to provide a bloodless field) Duration: 2–3 hours
documents.s: WALANT (Wide Awake Local Anaesthesia No Tourniquet)

documents.t: No sedation, no tourniquet, no block-related motor weakness Patient can demonstrate active finger flexion intraoperatively to confirm complete decompression Very low complication rate Cost-effective — can be performed outside main theatre

Median nerve block at the wrist — 3–5 mL lidocaine 2% between palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis at the proximal wrist crease Axillary brachial plexus block — if bilateral or combined with other hand procedures IV regional anaesthesia (Bier's block) — effective but requires tourniquet and monitoring setup

Median nerve enters the hand deep to the flexor retinaculum between tendons of FDS and FCR Palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the carpal tunnel — not protected by the retinaculum Recurrent motor branch to thenar muscles — variable anatomy, at risk during surgery

WALANT is now the standard of care in many centres for carpal tunnel release Adrenaline in fingers is safe — the old teaching that it causes digital ischaemia has been disproven No need for preoperative fasting with WALANT (no sedation) Bilateral procedures can be done in one sitting
NYSORA: Wrist Block Lalonde DH. Hand Clin. 2015 Nabhan A et al. J Hand Surg Eur. 2020
Open reduction and internal fixation of distal radius fractures — volar locking plate via a volar (Henry) approach. One of the most common orthopaedic procedures.

Targets: Trunks/divisions of the brachial plexus at the supraclavicular fossa Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial, needle directed toward the "corner pocket" (junction of first rib and subclavian artery) Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Onset: 10–15 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Axillary brachial plexus block — avoids risk of pneumothorax and phrenic nerve palsy; ideal when these are concerns Distal nerve blocks at the wrist — median, ulnar, and radial nerve blocks for targeted analgesia without proximal motor block; useful for postoperative analgesia alone (not suitable as primary surgical anaesthetic for ORIF)

Supraclavicular fossa: brachial plexus seen as a cluster of hypoechoic nodules ("bunch of grapes") lateral and posterior to the subclavian artery, above the first rib Pleura visible deep to the first rib — key safety landmark Dorsal scapular artery may traverse the plexus

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications High patient satisfaction; good pain control; increased day case rates Horner's (supraclavicular only); phrenic nerve block (supraclavicular only); tourniquet pain Pneumothorax (supraclavicular and infraclavicular)

Supraclavicular block provides reliable hand and wrist anaesthesia — "the spinal of the upper limb" Tourniquet tolerance is usually good for forearm tourniquets Avoid supraclavicular approach in patients with significant respiratory disease (phrenic nerve palsy risk ~1%) May include "top-up blocks" if incomplete; follow-up via text message service Can be performed as day-case regional anaesthesia without general anaesthesia
NYSORA: Supraclavicular Brachial Plexus Block NYSORA: Wrist Block Tran DQ et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2009
Elbow arthroscopy, ORIF of distal humerus/olecranon fractures, total elbow replacement, tennis elbow release, ulnar nerve transposition. Surgery may involve a tourniquet on the upper arm.

Targets: Trunks/divisions of the brachial plexus at the supraclavicular fossa — provides reliable coverage of the entire upper limb below the shoulder Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL levobupivacaine 0.5% Onset: 10–15 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Interscalene brachial plexus block — covers the elbow well (C5–C7) but may miss ulnar nerve (C8–T1) territory. Useful when shoulder and elbow are both involved. Risk of phrenic nerve palsy. Infraclavicular brachial plexus block — reliable coverage of all terminal branches. Good option for catheter placement. Axillary brachial plexus block — targets individual terminal nerves; no pneumothorax risk. May be preferred if the surgical site is distal on the forearm/elbow.

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Improved day case rate; athletic/sports injury patients may wish to watch surgery Horner's syndrome (supraclavicular/interscalene); phrenic nerve block (supraclavicular/interscalene); tourniquet pain Pneumothorax (supraclavicular and infraclavicular)

Elbow joint innervation: musculocutaneous, median, radial, and ulnar nerves Supraclavicular block reliably covers all four nerves Interscalene block may spare the inferior trunk (C8–T1) — ulnar nerve territory (medial elbow, little finger) Intercostobrachial nerve (T2) provides medial upper arm sensation — not covered by brachial plexus block; may need supplementation if an upper arm tourniquet is used

Supraclavicular is often called the "spinal of the upper limb" — most reliable single-injection technique for complete arm coverage For ulnar nerve surgery, ensure the block provides adequate ulnar nerve coverage — supraclavicular or infraclavicular approaches are more reliable than interscalene for this Upper arm tourniquet tolerance may require intercostobrachial nerve supplement or sedation Patients can often be discharged with a functioning block — provide written information on limb protection
NYSORA: Supraclavicular Brachial Plexus Block NYSORA: Infraclavicular Brachial Plexus Block Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)

Emergency and elective hand surgery — tendon repair, fracture fixation, nerve repair, replantation, debridement, skin grafting, flap coverage. Often performed as day case under regional anaesthesia alone.

Targets: Terminal branches of the brachial plexus — median, ulnar, radial, and musculocutaneous nerves Approach: Ultrasound-guided, at the axilla, identifying individual nerves around the axillary artery Local anaesthetic: 20–30 mL levobupivacaine 0.25–0.5% (divided between individual nerves) Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 8–16 hours

No risk of pneumothorax or phrenic nerve palsy Individual nerves can be selectively targeted — useful when only specific distributions are needed Well tolerated awake — ideal for day case surgery May include "top-up blocks" if coverage is incomplete
documents.s: Advantages of axillary block for hand trauma

documents.t: Supraclavicular brachial plexus block — provides reliable hand and forearm anaesthesia ("spinal of the upper limb"). Small risk of phrenic nerve palsy (~1%) and pneumothorax. Infraclavicular brachial plexus block — good for catheter placement if prolonged surgery or postoperative infusion is anticipated. Pneumothorax risk slightly higher than axillary. WALANT — for simple procedures (e.g. trigger finger, carpal tunnel, tendon repair) where no tourniquet is needed. See Carpal Tunnel Release. Wrist blocks — targeted median, ulnar, and/or radial nerve blocks at the wrist for limited procedures. Digital nerve blocks — for single finger procedures.

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications High patient satisfaction; good pain control; increased day case rates; follow-up via text message service Horner's (supraclavicular only); phrenic nerve block (supraclavicular only); tourniquet pain Pneumothorax (supraclavicular and infraclavicular)

Axillary block: median nerve lies superficial/lateral to axillary artery, ulnar nerve superficial/medial, radial nerve deep/posterior, musculocutaneous nerve within coracobrachialis Intercostobrachial nerve (T2) — provides sensation to medial upper arm; not covered by brachial plexus block. Requires separate subcutaneous infiltration if tourniquet is used on the upper arm.

Axillary block is the safest brachial plexus approach — no pneumothorax, no phrenic palsy For complex or prolonged hand surgery, consider an infraclavicular catheter for ongoing analgesia Tourniquet tolerance: forearm tourniquet is well tolerated under brachial plexus block; upper arm tourniquet may require intercostobrachial nerve supplement or sedation Patients can often be discharged with a functioning block — provide written information on limb protection and analgesia for the "wear-off" period
NYSORA: Supraclavicular Brachial Plexus Block NYSORA: Wrist Block Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)
Arthroscopic shoulder surgery — rotator cuff repair, subacromial decompression, labral repair, stabilisation procedures. Usually performed in the beach-chair or lateral decubitus position.

Targets: C5, C6 (± C7) nerve roots in the interscalene groove Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.375–0.5% Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours (with dexamethasone adjuvant)

Superior trunk block — reduced phrenic nerve palsy risk, similar analgesia for arthroscopic procedures Suprascapular nerve block — useful when interscalene is contraindicated (e.g. contralateral phrenic nerve palsy)
Interscalene groove between anterior and middle scalene muscles C5/C6 roots seen as hypoechoic round structures ("traffic light" sign) Phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of anterior scalene
Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Improved day case rate; athletic/sports injury patients may wish to watch surgery Horner's syndrome; phrenic nerve block Pneumothorax
Detail Needle Facet needle (Pajunk) Catheter Straight Pump Elastomeric Infusion Ropivacaine 0.2% — max 4 mL/hr (> 50 kg), 6 mL/hr (> 60 kg), 6 mL/hr (> 70 kg) Duration 72 hours

Phrenic nerve palsy occurs in ~100% of traditional interscalene blocks — avoid in patients with significant respiratory compromise or contralateral phrenic palsy Consider low-volume technique (5–10 mL) to reduce phrenic involvement Catheter placement for open procedures or anticipated severe pain May involve day case shoulder surgery under regional anaesthesia alone
NYSORA: Interscalene Brachial Plexus Block PROSPECT guidelines for shoulder surgery Tran DQ et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2019

Creation of arteriovenous fistula (AVF) or arteriovenous graft (AVG) for haemodialysis access — typically radiocephalic (wrist), brachiocephalic (elbow), or brachiobasilic (upper arm). Regional anaesthesia provides excellent operating conditions and may improve fistula maturation through sympathetic blockade and vasodilatation.

Targets: Trunks/divisions of the brachial plexus at the supraclavicular fossa Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL levobupivacaine 0.5% Onset: 10–15 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Sympathetic blockade causes vasodilatation — improves vessel calibre and may improve surgical outcomes and fistula patency Good pain control intraoperatively and postoperatively Potential for improved surgical outcomes pertaining to fistula creation Increased day case rates May include "top-up blocks" if incomplete

Axillary brachial plexus block — avoids phrenic nerve palsy risk; good for forearm AVF. May provide less consistent sympathetic blockade than supraclavicular. Infraclavicular brachial plexus block — good alternative, especially for upper arm procedures. Local infiltration — surgeon-delivered, for straightforward cases in patients who decline or are unsuitable for regional.

Benefits Common side effects Serious complications Good pain control; potential for improved surgical outcomes pertaining to fistula creation; increased day case rates Horner's (supraclavicular only); phrenic nerve block (supraclavicular only) Pneumothorax (supraclavicular and infraclavicular)

Supraclavicular fossa: brachial plexus as a cluster of hypoechoic nodules lateral and posterior to the subclavian artery, above the first rib Sympathetic fibres travel with the brachial plexus — blockade produces vasodilation and increased blood flow to the limb Radial artery and cephalic vein (wrist fistula) or brachial artery and cephalic/basilic veins (elbow/upper arm fistula) are the surgical targets

These patients are often high-risk (chronic kidney disease, diabetes, cardiovascular disease) — regional anaesthesia avoids the haemodynamic instability of general anaesthesia Many dialysis patients are anticoagulated — check timing with Anticoagulation & Blocks guidance. Supraclavicular is classified as a superficial block and can generally be performed without cessation of anticoagulants. Follow-up via text message service Avoid blood pressure measurements and venepuncture on the fistula arm
NYSORA: Supraclavicular Brachial Plexus Block Local Guideline for Peripheral Nerve Blockade in Adults (v2, October 2023)
